name: keyring (Python System Keyring Access)
pricing_notes: Open source (MIT); jaraco / Jason R. Coombs

auth.notes: Accesses OS keyring — macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, Linux Secret Service

agent_readiness.notes: Access OS credential storage from Python. import keyring. keyring.set_password('service', 'username', 'password'). password = keyring.get_password('service', 'username'). keyring.delete_password('service', 'username'). keyring.get_credential('service', 'username') → Credential(username, password). Backends: macOS Keychain (macOS), Windows Credential Manager (Win), Kwallet/SecretService (Linux), fail (no backend). CLI: keyring set service username, keyring get service username. Custom backends: class MyBackend(keyring.backend.KeyringBackend). keyring.core.set_keyring(backend). Used by pip, twine, AWS CLI for credential storage. keyring-alt for additional backends. SecretStorage for DBus/Linux.

gotchas: Linux requires SecretService (GNOME Keyring or KWallet) running — not available in all environments
Headless/CI environments often have no keyring backend — use keyrings.alt fallback
Service and username are case-sensitive on macOS, case-insensitive on Windows
Some CI systems need keyrings.alt or environment variables instead
best_when: Storing user credentials securely in desktop or developer tools
CLI tools that store API tokens per user (pip, twine pattern)
Avoiding hardcoded credentials in scripts or config files
macOS/Windows developer tools needing Keychain/Credential Manager
avoid_when: Server-side applications (use environment variables or secrets managers)
Headless CI/CD (use environment variables or vault)
When HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager are available
summary: keyring provides secure OS credential storage for Python. keyring.set_password('service', 'user', 'pass'); keyring.get_password('service', 'user'). macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, Linux SecretService. CLI: keyring set/get. Used by pip, twine, AWS CLI. MIT by jaraco. Linux needs SecretService running — not for headless servers. Best for desktop CLI tools needing per-user credential storage. Use environment variables or vaults for server-side.
